ac:rich-text-body.p: Subarrays are independent partitions of the SKA array that can be configured and scheduled separately. By dividing the full array into subarrays, the SKA can support multiple concurrent observations with different frequencies, sensitivities, and science requirements.

p: A subarray is a logically independent subset of the SKA's dishes (SKA-Mid) or stations (SKA-Low) that operates as a self-contained imaging instrument. Key characteristics:

ul.li: Independent configuration: Each subarray can be tuned to a different frequency band, with its own receiver setup and backend configuration
Concurrent observations: Multiple subarrays can observe simultaneously without interfering with one another
Flexible allocation: Subarrays can be created and dissolved on timescales from minutes to hours, allowing dynamic adjustment to observing schedules
Shared infrastructure: All subarrays share common facilities (power, fiber optics, correlator/beamformer resources) but operate independently in terms of pointing and data processing
Simultaneous multi-frequency observations: One subarray observing at 100 MHz while another observes at 1 GHz
Flexible scheduling: Different science programmes can observe different targets without waiting for array reconfiguration
Rapid target-of-opportunity response: A small, rapidly configurable subarray can respond to transient alerts while the main array continues scheduled observations
Array testing and maintenance: Commissioning and maintenance can be conducted on portions of the array while science continues elsewhere
Optimized sensitivity: Large subarrays achieve maximum sensitivity for faint-source surveys; smaller subarrays are better for bright, extended sources

p: SKA-Mid (197 dishes total) can be partitioned into subarrays with the following typical configurations:

tr.td: Maximum sensitivity surveys and deep fields

p: SKA-Low (512 stations total) can be partitioned as follows:

tr.td: Maximum sensitivity HI surveys and spectral-line

p: Practical constraints govern subarray creation and operation:

ul.li: Minimum size: Each subarray must contain a minimum number of dishes/stations (typically ~10–20 elements) to achieve acceptable imaging performance and redundancy

Frequency restrictions: Subarrays tuned to the same frequency band use the same receiver, so only one observation per frequency band per correlator/beamformer back-end can occur simultaneously. Different frequency bands can observe in parallel.
Correlator capacity: The central correlator (or beamformer) has finite processing capability. The total computational load across all subarrays cannot exceed available resources.
Data transport: High data rates from large subarrays or wide-bandwidth observations consume fiber-optic and compute resources that limit the total number of concurrent subarrays
Reconfiguration time: Moving dishes/stations between subarrays requires physical tuning (antenna motions, cable changes). Reconfiguration typically takes 5–30 minutes depending on scope of changes.
Observing blocks: Each science programme is scheduled as one or more observing blocks (OBs). An OB specifies subarray composition, target, duration, and technical parameters.
Array state: The scheduler tracks which dishes/stations are currently allocated to which subarray and what reconfiguration is needed between OBs.
Contention: When multiple programmes request the same subset of dishes, scheduling algorithms balance fairness, scientific priority, and operational efficiency.
Maintenance windows: Subarrays can be dynamically reconfigured to isolate equipment under maintenance, allowing continued observations on unaffected portions of the array.

p: The SKA's architecture supports dynamic reconfiguration, allowing subarrays to be adjusted during an observation block or between blocks:

ul.li: Frequency changes: If two subarrays are observing different (non-overlapping) frequency bands, they can coexist indefinitely.
Dish reallocation: Dishes can be moved between subarrays on timescales of minutes (for SKA-Mid, typical ~10 min reconfiguration). This enables rapid response to high-priority targets.
Load balancing: If one subarray's correlator load drops (e.g., observation ends early), freed capacity can be allocated to another subarray.
Beam hopping: Within a single subarray, the phased-array can rapidly switch between multiple pointing directions, enabling efficient surveys.

ac:rich-text-body.p: Requested subarray configurations are not guaranteed if they conflict with ongoing observations or maintenance. Proposers should indicate flexibility in subarray size and composition in their proposals. The SKA Time Allocation Committee will advise on the achievable subarray allocation for approved programmes.

ul.li: Minimum subarray size: The smallest subarray with which your science goals can be achieved
Preferred subarray configuration: Your ideal allocation if available
Frequency band: The receiver band(s) required
Integration time and sensitivity: How observing time and subarray size trade off in your science

p: The SKA User Support team can advise on realistic subarray allocations for your programme during proposal preparation.

ac:rich-text-body.p: Proposals that allow flexibility in subarray composition are more likely to be accommodated in the schedule and may receive priority in time allocation. Consider whether your science is robust to modest changes in sensitivity or resolution.
